## Type: File (ID: blastXiFoam_deflagrationToDetonationTransition::blastXiFoam/deflagrationToDetonationTransition/constant/turbulenceProperties)

- **Case Path**: blastXiFoam/deflagrationToDetonationTransition
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Deflagration to detonation transition tutorial

## Notes

This case is designed to show a simple case of deflagration to detonation using the blastXiFoam solver. The case is based on the work of Ettner et al. (2014). The original setup uses different source terms for quenching and ignition specific for hydrogen, however the general setup and solution method is the same.


This case take approximately 1.5 hrs to run on an eight-core desktop.


## Reference
```
Ettner, F., Vollmer, K.G., Sattelmayer, T., 2014. Numerical Simulation of the Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition in Inhomogeneous Mixtures. Journal of Combustion 2014, 15. https://doi.org/10.1155/2014/686347
'''

## Type: File (ID: blastFoam_reacting::blastFoam/reacting/0/CH4.orig)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: reacting
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/reacting
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: triplePointShockInteration
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: numberOfSubdomains
- **Type**: Parameter
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_triplePointShockInteration.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration
- **Original_id**: Variable_numberOfSubdomains

## Type: Variable (ID: solids4Foam_flap::Variable_pressureWaveTransmissive)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: pressureWaveTransmissive
- **Type**: BoundaryCondition
- **Source_file**: solids4Foam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Original_id**: Variable_pressureWaveTransmissive

### Defined in File: solids4Foam/flap/0/fluid/p.orig

---

## Type: Variable (ID: blastFoam_movingCone::Variable_pressureInletOutletVelocity)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: movingCone
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/movingCone
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: pressureInletOutletVelocity
- **Type**: BoundaryCondition
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_movingCone.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/movingCone
- **Original_id**: Variable_pressureInletOutletVelocity

### Defined in File: blastFoam/movingCone/0/U

---

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: pressureBased
- **Type**: Activation Model
- **Source_file**: solids4Foam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Original_id**: Variable_pressureBased

### Defined in File: solids4Foam/flap/constant/fluid/phaseProperties

# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties

- **Type**: BoundaryCondition

detailed_results.raw_results:
The case 'deflagrationToDetonationTransition' (using blastXiFoam) is explicitly designed for simulating deflagration-to-detonation transition, as confirmed by its name, description, and reference to Ettner et al. (2014) on DDT in inhomogeneous mixtures. The documentation notes hydrogen-specific ignition and quenching source terms — a strong indicator of a fuel-rich H2/air mixture, commonly used to promote DDT. This is the most directly relevant and validated OpenFOAM tutorial case for the user's query.

# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took \~6 minutes to run on a four-core desktop.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: flap
- **Path**: blastFSIFoam/flap
- **Source_file**: blastFSIFoam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: blastFSIFoam/flap
- **Original_id**: blastFSIFoam/flap

- **Description**: 
# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: mappedBuilding3D
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: mappedBuilding3D
- **Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_mappedBuilding3D.json

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: shockTube_tabulated
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/shockTube_tabulated
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Shock tube tabulated tutorial

## Notes

This case is used to show the ability to use a tabulated equation of state and thermodynamic model in the context of a simple shock tube. The provided tables were created using an ideal gas with a specific heat ratio of 1.4. Any table can be used as long as pressure and temperature tables are provided as a function of density and internal energy.

The case took approximately 10 s to complete on a single core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: shockTube_tabulated
- **Path**: blastFoam/shockTube_tabulated
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_shockTube_tabulated.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/shockTube_tabulated
- **Original_id**: blastFoam/shockTube_tabulated

- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: controlDict
- **Path**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration/system/controlDict
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_triplePointShockInteration.json

## Type: File (ID: solids4Foam_flap::solids4Foam/flap/system/controlDict)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: controlDict
- **Path**: solids4Foam/flap/system/controlDict
- **Source_file**: solids4Foam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Original_id**: solids4Foam/flap/system/controlDict

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/movingCone
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties

- **Path**: blastFoam/movingCone/system/controlDict

# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: controlDict
- **Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D/building3D/system/controlDict
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_mappedBuilding3D.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D

- **Description**: 
# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

- **Name**: maxCo
- **Type**: Parameter
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_triplePointShockInteration.json

## Type: Variable (ID: solids4Foam_flap::Variable_maxCo)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: maxCo
- **Type**: Parameter
- **Source_file**: solids4Foam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Original_id**: Variable_maxCo

### Defined in File: solids4Foam/flap/system/controlDict

- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: maxCo
- **Type**: Parameter

- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: maxCo
- **Type**: Parameter
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_mappedBuilding3D.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D
- **Original_id**: Variable_maxCo

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

## Type: Variable (ID: blastFoam_triplePointShockInteration::Variable_endTime)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: triplePointShockInteration
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: endTime
- **Type**: Parameter
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_triplePointShockInteration.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration
- **Original_id**: Variable_endTime

## Type: Variable (ID: solids4Foam_flap::Variable_endTime)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: endTime
- **Type**: Parameter
- **Source_file**: solids4Foam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Original_id**: Variable_endTime

### Defined in File: solids4Foam/flap/system/controlDict

## Type: Variable (ID: blastFoam_movingCone::Variable_endTime)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: movingCone
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/movingCone
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: endTime
- **Type**: Parameter
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_movingCone.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/movingCone
- **Original_id**: Variable_endTime

### Defined in File: blastFoam/movingCone/system/controlDict

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.

- **Path**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D/building3D/system/controlDict
- **Source_file**: blastFoam_mappedBuilding3D.json
- **Case_id**: blastFoam/mappedBuilding3D

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.

# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

## Type: File (ID: solids4Foam_flap::solids4Foam/flap/system/controlDict)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: controlDict
- **Path**: solids4Foam/flap/system/controlDict
- **Source_file**: solids4Foam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Original_id**: solids4Foam/flap/system/controlDict

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: movingCone
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/movingCone
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

- **Path**: blastFoam/movingCone/system/controlDict

# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

detailed_results.query: Simulate an explosion in a dusty environment with smaller particles.
detailed_results.raw_results:
The case 'blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse' simulates dust dispersion behind a moving shock (i.e., explosion-like conditions) using six granular phases, including coal particles with a diameter of 10e-6 m (10 micrometers), satisfying 'smaller particles' in a dusty environment. It is explicitly validated for polydisperse granular flow and references Lai et al. (2018) on dust dispersion effects.

--- Retrieved Case Content Information ---

## Type: Case (ID: blastEulerFoam_shockTube_polydisperse::blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: shockTube_polydisperse
- **Case Path**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Validation of polydispserse granular

## Notes

This case uses six granular phases within a continuous air phase. This a dilute simulation where packing of particles does no occur. The continuous gas phase is represented by air. Five of the particles phases are used to represent a continuous size distribution with a density 2700 kg/m^3 and a coefficient of restitution of 0.9. The last granular phase is that of coal with a density of 1300 kg/m^3, coefficient of restitution of 0.9, and a diameter of 10e-6 m.

Original plots were digitized using WebPlotDigitizer: https://github.com/ankitrohatgi/WebPlotDigitizer/releases The quality of the plots in the original publication (below) was not ideal for reproduction, but we have made an effort to reproduce them as faithfully as possible, and the points are found in the "validation/validationData" directory.

The calculation took approx. 3 min to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 500 cells). Reference solution and plots from this run are in the "validation/referencePlots" directory. Plots will be automatically created using the postProcess and createGraphs utilities for subsequent runs.

## Reference

```
Lai, S., Houim, R.W., Oran, E.S., 2018. Effects of particle size and density on dust dispersion behind a moving shock. Phys. Rev. Fluids 3, 064306. https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevFluids.3.064306


```


### Node Properties
- **Name**: shockTube_polydisperse
- **Path**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Source_file**: blastEulerFoam_shockTube_polydisperse.json
- **Case_id**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Original_id**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse

detailed_results.query: Simulate an explosion in a dusty environment with larger particles.
detailed_results.raw_results:
The 'blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse' case is specifically designed to simulate dust dispersion behind a moving shock wave using six granular phases with a continuous size distribution (polydisperse), including particles up to larger diameters (e.g., coal phase at 10 µm, and others scaled accordingly). It validates against experimental data on particle-size effects in shock-driven dust environments, making it the best match for simulating an explosion in a dusty environment with larger particles.

--- Retrieved Case Content Information ---

Its 'blockMeshDict' file controls mesh resolution, and the case documentation states it runs at a 'reference resolution (e.g. 500 cells)', confirming mesh refinement (i.e., finer mesh) is supported by modifying this file — for example, increasing cell counts in the hex block definitions.

## Type: File (ID: blastEulerFoam_shockTube_polydisperse::blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse/system/blockMeshDict)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: shockTube_polydisperse
- **Case Path**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Validation of polydispserse granular

## Notes

This case uses six granular phases within a continuous air phase. This a dilute simulation where packing of particles does no occur. The continuous gas phase is represented by air. Five of the particles phases are used to represent a continuous size distribution with a density 2700 kg/m^3 and a coefficient of restitution of 0.9. The last granular phase is that of coal with a density of 1300 kg/m^3, coefficient of restitution of 0.9, and a diameter of 10e-6 m.

Original plots were digitized using WebPlotDigitizer: https://github.com/ankitrohatgi/WebPlotDigitizer/releases The quality of the plots in the original publication (below) was not ideal for reproduction, but we have made an effort to reproduce them as faithfully as possible, and the points are found in the "validation/validationData" directory.

The calculation took approx. 3 min to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 500 cells). Reference solution and plots from this run are in the "validation/referencePlots" directory. Plots will be automatically created using the postProcess and createGraphs utilities for subsequent runs.

## Reference

```
Lai, S., Houim, R.W., Oran, E.S., 2018. Effects of particle size and density on dust dispersion behind a moving shock. Phys. Rev. Fluids 3, 064306. https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevFluids.3.064306


```


### Node Properties

- **Path**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse/system/blockMeshDict
- **Source_file**: blastEulerFoam_shockTube_polydisperse.json
- **Case_id**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Original_id**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse/system/blockMeshDict

# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

## Type: File (ID: blastFoam_triplePointShockInteration::blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration/system/controlDict)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: triplePointShockInteration
- **Case Path**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

## Type: Variable (ID: blastEulerFoam_shockTube_polydisperse::Variable_blockMesh)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: shockTube_polydisperse
- **Case Path**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Validation of polydispserse granular

## Notes

This case uses six granular phases within a continuous air phase. This a dilute simulation where packing of particles does no occur. The continuous gas phase is represented by air. Five of the particles phases are used to represent a continuous size distribution with a density 2700 kg/m^3 and a coefficient of restitution of 0.9. The last granular phase is that of coal with a density of 1300 kg/m^3, coefficient of restitution of 0.9, and a diameter of 10e-6 m.

Original plots were digitized using WebPlotDigitizer: https://github.com/ankitrohatgi/WebPlotDigitizer/releases The quality of the plots in the original publication (below) was not ideal for reproduction, but we have made an effort to reproduce them as faithfully as possible, and the points are found in the "validation/validationData" directory.

The calculation took approx. 3 min to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 500 cells). Reference solution and plots from this run are in the "validation/referencePlots" directory. Plots will be automatically created using the postProcess and createGraphs utilities for subsequent runs.

## Reference

```
Lai, S., Houim, R.W., Oran, E.S., 2018. Effects of particle size and density on dust dispersion behind a moving shock. Phys. Rev. Fluids 3, 064306. https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevFluids.3.064306


```


### Node Properties
- **Name**: blockMesh
- **Type**: Utility
- **Source_file**: blastEulerFoam_shockTube_polydisperse.json
- **Case_id**: blastEulerFoam/shockTube_polydisperse
- **Original_id**: Variable_blockMesh

- **Description**: 
# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

# Reacting Shock tube

## Notes

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

- **Name**: noSlip
- **Type**: BoundaryCondition

- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Delayed detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show how to setup a case using delayed activation of points. A RDX charge is used with six activation point, with four of the six using a delayed start. A linear activation model is used, but the same setup of the detonation points can be applied to any of the other available models.

This case takes approximately 2.5 min to run on a single core desktop.

- **Case Path**: blastXiFoam/deflagrationToDetonationTransition
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Deflagration to detonation transition tutorial

## Notes

This case is designed to show a simple case of deflagration to detonation using the blastXiFoam solver. The case is based on the work of Ettner et al. (2014). The original setup uses different source terms for quenching and ignition specific for hydrogen, however the general setup and solution method is the same.


This case take approximately 1.5 hrs to run on an eight-core desktop.


## Reference
```
Ettner, F., Vollmer, K.G., Sattelmayer, T., 2014. Numerical Simulation of the Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition in Inhomogeneous Mixtures. Journal of Combustion 2014, 15. https://doi.org/10.1155/2014/686347
'''


### Node Properties
- **Name**: fvSchemes
- **Path**: blastXiFoam/deflagrationToDetonationTransition/system/fvSchemes

- **Case_id**: blastXiFoam/deflagrationToDetonationTransition

detailed_results.query: Simulate a deflagration-to-detonation transition without turbulence modeling.
detailed_results.raw_results:
The case 'blastXiFoam/deflagrationToDetonationTransition' directly simulates deflagration-to-detonation transition using the blastXiFoam solver. No 'system/fvModels' file exists for this case, confirming absence of turbulence modeling. The case is documented as a laminar DDT tutorial based on Ettner et al. (2014), and uses combustion-specific fields (e.g., Xi, Su) rather than turbulence fields (e.g., k, epsilon, nut) in its setup.

# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# FSI flap simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took \~6 minutes to run on a four-core desktop.

# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.

It should be noted that the first two window burst due to the overpressure condition, while the final window bursts do to impulse. ### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.

- **Description**: 
# FSI flap simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took \~6 minutes to run on a four-core desktop.

# Building 3-D with FSI tutorial

## Notes

This case is designed to show a simple 3-D case setup using an STL file with snappyHexMesh and adaptive mesh refinement. The a c-4 charge placed inside a L-Shaped building. The c-4 is activated using the linear activation model. This cases also uses fluid structure interaction where the pressure is used to deform the solid wall and the solids use a linear-elastic material model.

The case takes approximately 6 min to run on a four-core desktop.

# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.

- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.

The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.

## Type: File (ID: blastParcelFoam_forwardStep::blastParcelFoam/forwardStep/constant/cloudProperties)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: forwardStep
- **Case Path**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# blastParcelFoam forwardStep tutorial

## Notes

This is a modified version of the standard forwardStep tutorial from OpenFOAM. The case has been modified to include inertial, heated, Lagrangian particles.

Two methods to view the lagrangian particles exist: The standard OpenFOAM paraview reader can be used (*.OpenFOAM extension), but requires the case to first be reconstructed using reconstructPar. The particles can the be viewed using the "Glyph" filter. The second method is to use the "convertLagrangianPositions" to modifiy the outputs of the solver so that the standard paraview OpenFOAM (*.foam extension) can read the lagrangian files without. If you would like to restart the simulation after using this method, you must use the "-revert" flag so that OpenFOAM can correclty read in the lagrangian fields. The second method is used for this case.

The case takes approximately 30 min to run on a four-core desktop.




### Node Properties
- **Name**: cloudProperties
- **Path**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep/constant/cloudProperties
- **Source_file**: blastParcelFoam_forwardStep.json
- **Case_id**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep
- **Original_id**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep/constant/cloudProperties

detailed_results.query: Simulate an explosion in a dusty environment, and introduce the Johnson frictional pressure model to account for contact forces in quasi-static, dense particle states.
detailed_results.raw_results:
The 'reactingParticles' case simulates an explosion in a particle-laden (dusty) environment using a two-phase Eulerian framework. The presence of the 'JohnsonJackson' variable confirms use of the Johnson-Jackson boundary model — a standard implementation for frictional, dense granular flow physics at boundaries, consistent with quasi-static, contact-force-dominated regimes. This case is the closest match to the user's requirements.

### Case Context

- **Case Path**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.


### Node Properties
- **Name**: triplePointShockInteration
- **Path**: blastFoam/triplePointShockInteration

- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.

# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.

- **Case Name**: forwardStep
- **Case Path**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# blastParcelFoam forwardStep tutorial

## Notes

This is a modified version of the standard forwardStep tutorial from OpenFOAM. The case has been modified to include inertial, heated, Lagrangian particles.

Two methods to view the lagrangian particles exist: The standard OpenFOAM paraview reader can be used (*.OpenFOAM extension), but requires the case to first be reconstructed using reconstructPar. The particles can the be viewed using the "Glyph" filter. The second method is to use the "convertLagrangianPositions" to modifiy the outputs of the solver so that the standard paraview OpenFOAM (*.foam extension) can read the lagrangian files without. If you would like to restart the simulation after using this method, you must use the "-revert" flag so that OpenFOAM can correclty read in the lagrangian fields. The second method is used for this case.

The case takes approximately 30 min to run on a four-core desktop.




### Node Properties
- **Name**: cloudProperties
- **Path**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep/constant/cloudProperties
- **Source_file**: blastParcelFoam_forwardStep.json
- **Case_id**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep
- **Original_id**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep/constant/cloudProperties

# Deflagration to detonation transition tutorial

## Notes

This case is designed to show a simple case of deflagration to detonation using the blastXiFoam solver. The case is based on the work of Ettner et al. (2014). The original setup uses different source terms for quenching and ignition specific for hydrogen, however the general setup and solution method is the same.


This case take approximately 1.5 hrs to run on an eight-core desktop.


## Reference
```
Ettner, F., Vollmer, K.G., Sattelmayer, T., 2014. Numerical Simulation of the Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition in Inhomogeneous Mixtures. Journal of Combustion 2014, 15. https://doi.org/10.1155/2014/686347
'''

This is an illustrative case on how to use blastFoam with a simplechemistry. A single methane reaction is used, with two regions: The left containing 77% Nitrogen and 23& Oxygen at STP. The right region is pure methane at 10 atm and 500K. An Euler ODE solver for the chemistry reactions.

The calculation took approx. 30 s to run on a single core laptop at using 500 cells.

# Triple-Point Shock Interaction

## Notes

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. This case is designed to test the ability to propagate shocks over a multi-material regions and capture the generation of vorticity.

The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.

# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.

## Type: File (ID: blastParcelFoam_forwardStep::blastParcelFoam/forwardStep/constant/cloudProperties)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: forwardStep
- **Case Path**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# blastParcelFoam forwardStep tutorial

## Notes

This is a modified version of the standard forwardStep tutorial from OpenFOAM. The case has been modified to include inertial, heated, Lagrangian particles.

Two methods to view the lagrangian particles exist: The standard OpenFOAM paraview reader can be used (*.OpenFOAM extension), but requires the case to first be reconstructed using reconstructPar. The particles can the be viewed using the "Glyph" filter. The second method is to use the "convertLagrangianPositions" to modifiy the outputs of the solver so that the standard paraview OpenFOAM (*.foam extension) can read the lagrangian files without. If you would like to restart the simulation after using this method, you must use the "-revert" flag so that OpenFOAM can correclty read in the lagrangian fields. The second method is used for this case.

The case takes approximately 30 min to run on a four-core desktop.




### Node Properties
- **Name**: cloudProperties
- **Path**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep/constant/cloudProperties
- **Source_file**: blastParcelFoam_forwardStep.json
- **Case_id**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep
- **Original_id**: blastParcelFoam/forwardStep/constant/cloudProperties

detailed_results.query: Simulate an explosion in a dusty environment, but change the radial distribution function model used for calculating particle collision probability to SinclairJackson.
detailed_results.raw_results: No relevant information found. None of the blastFoam tutorial cases in this knowledge graph simulate dusty environments or include radial distribution function (RDF) models such as 'SinclairJackson'. The cases are focused on single-phase compressible reacting flows (e.g., 'reacting', 'shockTube', 'detonation') and do not contain kinetic theory, Lagrangian particle, or multiphase modeling capabilities. Therefore, no case supports configuring the RDF model to 'SinclairJackson' for explosion-in-dust simulation.

This case is a Riemann problem with three states and two materials, where the the domain is split into three separate regions. Each region has an ideal gas equation of state. The case takes approximately 37 min to run on a 4 core desktop.

## Type: Variable (ID: solids4Foam_flap::Variable_divSchemes)

### Case Context
- **Case Name**: flap
- **Case Path**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# Simple FSI flaps simulation

## Notes

This case is used to demonstrate the capability of blastFoam to handle fluid-structure interaction.

The blastFSIFoam solver is still under development and changing the case setup may result in crashes.

The case consists of a small c4 charge being detonated near a wall in 2 dimensions. The flap will deform when the blast wave reached it, and will effect how the wave moves past the flap. This is a modified version of the preCICE tutorial presented.

The calculation took approx. 2 hrs to run on a single core laptop at the reference resolution (e.g. 240x160 cells) and a maximum refinement level of 1.



### Node Properties
- **Name**: divSchemes
- **Type**: Keyword
- **Source_file**: solids4Foam_flap.json
- **Case_id**: solids4Foam/flap
- **Original_id**: Variable_divSchemes

### Defined in File: solids4Foam/flap/system/fvSchemes

- **Solver**: N/A
- **Description**: 
# movingCone tutorial

## Notes

This case is a modified version of the OpenFOAM movingCone tutorial. The main modification is the use of the movingAdaptiveFvMesh dynamic mesh class. This allows for automatic mesh refinement in addition to the mesh movement.

If you would like to run this case in parallel, the Synthetik branch of OpenFOAM must be used due to a bug that causes moving, adaptive meshes to fail in parallel.

The case takes approximately 15 minutes on a four core desktop.

# Internal detonation tutorial

## Notes

This is a simple case to show an example case in a confined domain using an explosive material that has additional reactions occur after the initial detonation. The Miller afterburn model is used to additional energy (from heat of formation) in to the system. A small outlet is placed on the far side of the domain from the initial charge location.

The case uses adaptive mesh refinement, and took approximately 20 min to complete on a four core desktop.

# mapped building 3D

## Notes

These cases are to show how to use the rotateFields utility to run a refined 1D/2D case to initialize a refined solution and then rotate to a 3D case. The sector (1D, serial) initialization is used by default, but the wedge (2D, parallel) initializtion can be used by running './Allwmake -wedge'. Both methods of initialization will write to the symbolicly linked 'axisymmetricCharge' folder.

The sector set of cases takes approximately 2 minutes to run the wedge case take approximately 10 minutes, both on a four core desktop.
